    17→import Mathlib.Data.Nat.Squarefree
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Primitive elements of a set with respect to divisibility
    21→
    22→An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ⊆ ℕ` if `n ∈ S` and no proper divisor of `n` is in `S`.
    23→
    24→This concept appears naturally when studying sequences closed under "divisor inheritance" -
    25→if every member `n` of a sequence has the property that `m * n` is also in the sequence
    26→for coprime `m`, then the primitive elements generate the entire sequence.
    27→
    28→## Main definitions
    29→
    30→* `Set.IsPrimitive`: A natural number `n` is primitive in `S` if `n ∈ S` and
    31→  `n.properDivisors` is disjoint from `S`.
    32→* `Set.primitives`: The set of primitive elements of `S`.
    33→
    34→## Main results
    35→
    36→* `Set.IsPrimitive.mem`: A primitive element is in the set.
    37→* `Set.IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor`: Proper divisors of a primitive element are not
    38→  in the set.
    39→* `Set.one_isPrimitive_iff`: `1` is primitive in `S` iff `1 ∈ S`.
    40→* `Set.prime_isPrimitive_iff`: A prime `p` is primitive in `S` iff `p ∈ S` and `1 ∉ S`.
    41→-/
    42→
    43→namespace Set
    44→
    45→/-- An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ⊆ ℕ` if `n ∈ S` and no proper divisor of `n`
    46→is in `S`. -/
    47→def IsPrimitive (S : Set ℕ) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    48→  n ∈ S ∧ Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set ℕ) S
    49→
    50→/-- The set of primitive elements of `S`. -/
    51→def primitives (S : Set ℕ) : Set ℕ :=
    52→  {n | S.IsPrimitive n}
    53→
    54→theorem IsPrimitive.mem {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) : n ∈ S := h.1
    55→
    56→theorem IsPrimitive.disjoint_properDivisors {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) :
    57→    Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set ℕ) S := h.2
    58→
    59→theorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor {S : Set ℕ} {n d : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    60→    (hd : d ∈ n.properDivisors) : d ∉ S :=
    61→  Set.disjoint_left.mp h.2 hd
    62→
    63→theorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_dvd_of_lt {S : Set ℕ} {n d : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    64→    (hdvd : d ∣ n) (hlt : d < n) : d ∉ S :=
    65→  h.not_mem_of_properDivisor (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mpr ⟨hdvd, hlt⟩)
    66→
    67→theorem isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} :
    68→    S.IsPrimitive n ↔ n ∈ S ∧ ∀ d ∈ n.properDivisors, d ∉ S := by
    69→  simp only [IsPrimitive, Set.disjoint_left, Finset.mem_coe]
    70→
    71→theorem isPrimitive_iff' {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} (_hn : 0 < n) :
    72→    S.IsPrimitive n ↔ n ∈ S ∧ ∀ d, d ∣ n → d < n → d ∉ S := by
    73→  rw [isPrimitive_iff]
    74→  constructor <;> intro ⟨hmem, hdiv⟩
    75→  · exact ⟨hmem, fun d hd hlt => hdiv d (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mpr ⟨hd, hlt⟩)⟩
    76→  · exact ⟨hmem, fun d hd => hdiv d (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd).1 (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd).2⟩
    77→
    78→theorem mem_primitives_iff {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} : n ∈ S.primitives ↔ S.IsPrimitive n := Iff.rfl
    79→
    80→@[simp]
    81→theorem primitives_subset {S : Set ℕ} : S.primitives ⊆ S := fun _ h => h.mem
    82→
    83→theorem one_isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ℕ} : S.IsPrimitive 1 ↔ 1 ∈ S := by
    84→  simp [isPrimitive_iff, Nat.properDivisors_one]
    85→
    86→theorem prime_isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ℕ} {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime) :
    87→    S.IsPrimitive p ↔ p ∈ S ∧ 1 ∉ S := by
    88→  simp [isPrimitive_iff, hp.properDivisors]
    89→
    90→end Set
    91→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 892
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/892](https://www.erdosproblems.com/892)
    23→
    24→Is there a necessary and sufficient condition for a sequence of integers
    25→$b_1 < b_2 < \cdots$ that ensures there exists a primitive sequence
    26→$a_1 < a_2 < \cdots$ (i.e. no element divides another) with $a_n \ll b_n$
    27→for all $n$?
    28→
    29→[ESS68] Erdős, P., Sárközy, A., and Szemerédi, E.
    30→
    31→[Er98] Erdős, P.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos892
    35→
    36→/-- A sequence of natural numbers is *primitive* if no element divides any other. -/
    37→def IsPrimitiveSeq (a : ℕ → ℕ) : Prop :=
    38→  ∀ i j, i ≠ j → ¬(a i ∣ a j)
    39→
    40→/--
    41→Erdős Problem 892 (particular case) [Er98]:
    42→
    43→If $b : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ is a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers such that
    44→$\gcd(b_i, b_j) \neq b_k$ for all pairwise distinct $i, j, k$ (no non-trivial GCD solutions),
    45→then there exists a strictly increasing primitive sequence $a : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ with
    46→$a_n \ll b_n$ (i.e. there exists $C$ such that $a_n \leq C \cdot b_n$ for all $n$).
    47→-/
    48→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    49→theorem erdos_892
    50→    (b : ℕ → ℕ)
    51→    (hb_pos : ∀ n, 0 < b n)
    52→    (hb_mono : StrictMono b)
    53→    (hb_gcd : ∀ i j k, i ≠ j → i ≠ k → j ≠ k →
    54→      Nat.gcd (b i) (b j) ≠ b k) :
    55→    ∃ (a : ℕ → ℕ) (C : ℕ),
    56→      0 < C ∧
    57→      StrictMono a ∧
    58→      IsPrimitiveSeq a ∧
    59→      ∀ n, a n ≤ C * b n := by
    60→  sorry
    61→
    62→/--
    63→Erdős Problem 892 (general form) [ESS68] [Er98]:
    64→
    65→Characterize which strictly increasing sequences of positive integers $b_1 < b_2 < \cdots$ have the
    66→property that there exists a primitive sequence $a_1 < a_2 < \cdots$ with $a_n \ll b_n$.
    67→-/
    68→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    69→theorem erdos_892.variants.general :
    70→    {b : ℕ → ℕ | (∀ n, 0 < b n) ∧ StrictMono b ∧
    71→      ∃ (a : ℕ → ℕ) (C : ℕ), 0 < C ∧ StrictMono a ∧ IsPrimitiveSeq a ∧
    72→        ∀ n, a n ≤ C * b n} = answer(sorry) := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→end Erdos892
    76→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 409
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/409](https://www.erdosproblems.com/409)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→open scoped Topology ArithmeticFunction.sigma Nat
    26→open Filter
    27→
    28→namespace Erdos409
    29→
    30→/--
    31→How many iterations of $n\mapsto\phi(n) + 1$ are needed before a prime is reached?
    32→-/
    33→-- Formalisation note: the sequence of iterates always terminates if `n > 0`
    34→-- since it is strictly decreasing unless the input is prime, at which point
    35→-- it becomes static. See also https://oeis.org/A39651
    36→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    37→theorem erdos_409.parts.i (n : ℕ) (hn : 0 < n) :
    38→    IsLeast { i | (φ · + 1)^[i] n |>.Prime } answer(sorry) := by
    39→  sorry
    40→
    41→/-- If $n > 0$, then the iteration $n\mapsto\phi(n) + 1$ necessarily
    42→reaches a prime. -/
    43→@[category test, AMS 11]
    44→theorem erdos_409.termination (n : ℕ) (hn : 0 < n) :
    45→    ∃ i, (φ · + 1)^[i] n |>.Prime := by
    46→  sorry
    47→
    48→-- Formalisation note: it's possible that solution to `erdos_409.parts.i` needs to be
    49→-- expressed asymptotically. To handle this we include `IsTheta`, `IsBigO`
    50→-- and `IsLittleO` variants below. Since a solution is not known this necessitates
    51→-- the use of an `answer(sorry)` placeholder. Trivial or sub-optimal solutions
    52→-- will therefore exist to the asymptotic formalisations. A true solution to
    53→-- the asymptotic variants should have a degree of optimality or non-triviality to it.
    54→/--
    55→Let $c(n)$ be the minimum number of iterations of $n\mapsto\phi(n) + 1$ before a prime
    56→is reached. What is $\Theta(c(n))$?
    57→-/
    58→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    59→theorem erdos_409.parts.i.variants.isTheta (c : ℕ → ℕ)
    60→    (h : ∀ n > 0, IsLeast { i | (φ · + 1)^[i] n |>.Prime } (c n)) :
    61→    (fun n => (c n : ℝ)) =Θ[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
    62→  sorry
    63→
    64→/--
    65→Let $c(n)$ be the minimum number of iterations of $n\mapsto\phi(n) + 1$ before a prime
    66→is reached. Find the simplest function $g(n)$ such that $c(n) = O(g(n))$?
    67→-/
    68→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    69→theorem erdos_409.parts.i.variants.isBigO (c : ℕ → ℕ)
    70→    (h : ∀ n > 0, IsLeast { i | (φ · + 1)^[i] n |>.Prime } (c n)) :
    71→    (fun n => (c n : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
    72→  sorry
    73→
    74→/--
    75→Let $c(n)$ be the minimum number of iterations of $n\mapsto\phi(n) + 1$ before a prime
    76→is reached. Find the simplest function $g(n)$ such that $c(n) = o(g(n))$?
    77→-/
    78→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    79→theorem erdos_409.parts.i.variants.isLittleO (c : ℕ → ℕ)
    80→    (h : ∀ n > 0, IsLeast { i | (φ · + 1)^[i] n |>.Prime } (c n)) :
    81→    (fun n => (c n : ℝ)) =o[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
    82→  sorry
    83→
    84→/--
    85→Can infinitely many $n$ reach the same prime under the iteration $n\mapsto\phi(n) + 1$?
    86→-/
    87→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    88→theorem erdos_409.parts.ii :
    89→    answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ (p : ℕ) (hp : p.Prime), { n | ∃ i, (φ · + 1)^[i] n = p }.Infinite := by
    90→  sorry
    91→
    92→/--
    93→What is the density of $n$ which reach any fixed prime under the iteration $n\mapsto\phi(n) + 1$?
    94→-/
    95→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    96→theorem erdos_409.parts.iii (p : ℕ) (h : p.Prime) (α : ℝ)
    97→    (hα : { n | ∃ i, (φ · + 1)^[i] n = p }.HasDensity α) :
    98→    α = answer(sorry) := by
    99→  sorry
   100→
   101→/--
   102→How many iterations of $n\mapsto\sigma(n) - 1$ are needed before a prime is reached?
   103→-/
   104→-- Formalisation note: non-termination of this sequence is less clear since
   105→-- it is strictly increasing except at primes.
   106→@[category research open, AMS 11]
   107→theorem erdos_409.variants.sigma.parts.i (n : ℕ) (hn : n > 1) :
   108→    IsLeast { i | (σ 1 · - 1)^[i] n |>.Prime } answer(sorry) := by
   109→  sorry
   110→
   111→/-- If $n > 1$ then the iteration $n\mapsto\sigma(n) - 1$ necessarily reaches a prime. -/
   112→@[category test, AMS 11]
   113→theorem erdos_409.variants.sigma.termination (n : ℕ) (hn : n > 1) :
   114→    ∃ i, (σ 1 · - 1)^[i] n |>.Prime := by
   115→  sorry
   116→
   117→-- Formalisation note: See the above formalisation note for the rationale
   118→-- for including asymptotic variants
   119→/--
   120→Let $c(n)$ be the minimum number of iterations of $n\mapsto\sigma(n) - 1$ before a prime
   121→is reached. What is $\Theta(c(n))$?
   122→-/
   123→@[category research open, AMS 11]
   124→theorem erdos_409.variants.sigma.parts.i.isTheta (c : ℕ → ℕ)
   125→    (h : ∀ n > 1, IsLeast { i | (σ 1 · - 1)^[i] n |>.Prime } (c n)) :
   126→    (fun n => (c n : ℝ)) =Θ[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
   127→  sorry
   128→
   129→/--

   131→is reached. Find the simplest function $g(n)$ such that $c(n) = O(g(n))$?
   132→-/

   134→theorem erdos_409.variants.sigma.parts.i.isBigO (c : ℕ → ℕ)
   135→    (h : ∀ n > 1, IsLeast { i | (σ 1 · - 1)^[i] n |>.Prime } (c n)) :

   142→-/

   144→theorem erdos_409.variants.sigma.parts.i.isLittleO (c : ℕ → ℕ)

   150→Is it true that iterates of $n\mapsto\sigma(n) - 1$ always reach a prime?

   153→theorem erdos_409.variants.sigma.parts.ii :

    24→-/
    25→
    26→open AdditiveCombinatorics Filter Real Set
    27→open scoped Pointwise
    28→
    29→namespace Erdos40
    30→
    31→/--
    32→The predicate for a function $g\colon\mathbb{N} → \mathbb{R})$ that
    33→$$\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert \gg \frac{N^{1/2}}{g(N)}$$
    34→implies $\limsup 1_A\ast 1_A(n)=\infty$.
    35→-/
    36→def Erdos40For (g : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop :=
    37→  ∀ A : Set ℕ,
    38→    (fun N : ℕ ↦ √N / g N) =O[atTop] (fun N ↦ ((A ∩ .Icc 1 N).ncard : ℝ)) →
    39→    limsup (fun N ↦ (sumRep A N : ℕ∞)) atTop = ⊤
    40→
    41→/--
    42→Given a set of functions $\mathbb{N} → \mathbb{R})$, we assert that for all $g$ in that set,
    43→if $g(N) → \infty$ then
    44→$$\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert \gg \frac{N^{1/2}}{g(N)}$$
    45→implies $\limsup 1_A\ast 1_A(n)=\infty$.
    46→-/
    47→def Erdos40ForSet (G : Set (ℕ → ℝ)) : Prop := ∀ g ∈ G, Tendsto g atTop atTop → Erdos40For g
    48→
    49→/--
    50→For what functions $g(N) → \infty$ is it true that
    51→$$\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert \gg \frac{N^{1/2}}{g(N)}$$
    52→implies $\limsup 1_A\ast 1_A(n)=\infty$?
    53→-/
    54→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    55→theorem erdos_40 : Erdos40ForSet answer(sorry) := by
    56→  sorry
    57→
    58→/--
    59→If we don't pose additional conditions on the functions, then this is a stronger form of the
    60→Erdős-Turán conjecture, see Erdõs Problem 28,
    61→(since establishing this for any function $g(N) → \infty$ would imply a positive solution to Erdős
    62→Problem 28).
    63→-/
    64→@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
    65→theorem erdos_28_of_erdos_40 (h_erdos_40 : Erdos40ForSet .univ) : type_of% Erdos28.erdos_28 := by
    66→  simp only [Erdos40ForSet, Erdos40For, sumRep, sumConv, indicatorOne, mem_univ, forall_const]
    67→    at h_erdos_40
    68→  intro A hA
    69→  apply h_erdos_40
    70→  rotate_right
    71→  · exact fun N => (N : ℝ).sqrt
    72→  · rw [funext Real.sqrt_eq_rpow]
    73→    exact (tendsto_rpow_atTop (one_half_pos)).comp (tendsto_natCast_atTop_atTop)
    74→  · have ⟨n, hn⟩ := hA.exists_le
    75→    apply Asymptotics.IsBigO.of_bound 1
    76→    apply Filter.eventually_atTop.mpr
    77→    use n + 1
    78→    intro m hm
    79→    have : 0 < m := by omega
    80→    field_simp
    81→    simp only [one_mem, CStarRing.norm_of_mem_unitary, RCLike.norm_natCast, Nat.one_le_cast]
    82→    apply Nat.card_pos_iff.mpr
    83→    constructor
    84→    · by_contra h_empty
    85→      have : m ∈ (A + A)ᶜ := by
    86→        intro h
    87→        replace ⟨a, ha, b, hb, h⟩ := h
    88→        absurd h_empty
    89→        by_cases ha' : 1 ≤ a
    90→        · refine ⟨a, ha, ha', by bound⟩
    91→        · exact ⟨b, hb, by simp only at h; omega, by bound⟩
    92→      have := hn m this
    93→      omega
    94→    · exact (Set.finite_Icc _ _).inter_of_right A
    95→
    96→end Erdos40
    97→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 319
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/319](https://www.erdosproblems.com/319)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→open Filter
    26→
    27→open scoped Topology Finset Real
    28→
    29→namespace Erdos319
    30→
    31→/-- What is the size of the largest $A\subseteq\{1, \dots, N\}$ such that there is a function
    32→$\delta : A \to \{-1, 1\}$ such that
    33→$$
    34→  \sum_{n\in A} \frac{\delta n}{n} = 0
    35→$$
    36→and
    37→$$
    38→  \sum_{n\in A'}\frac{\delta n}{n} \neq 0
    39→$$
    40→for all non-empty $A'\subsetneq A$. -/
    41→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    42→theorem erdos_319 (N : ℕ) : IsGreatest
    43→    { #A | (A) (_ : A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N)
    44→      (_ : ∃ δ : ℕ → ℤˣ, ∑ n ∈ A, (δ n : ℚ) / n = 0 ∧
    45→        ∀ A' ⊂ A, A'.Nonempty → ∑ n ∈ A', (δ n : ℚ) / n ≠ 0) }
    46→    answer(sorry) := by
    47→  sorry
    48→
    49→-- Formalisation note: it's possible that solution to `erdos_319` needs to be
    50→-- expressed asymptotically. To handle this we include `IsTheta`, `IsBigO`
    51→-- and `IsLittleO` variants below. Since a solution is not known this necessitates
    52→-- the use of an `answer(sorry)` placeholder. Trivial or sub-optimal solutions
    53→-- will therefore exist to the asymptotic formalisations. A true solution to
    54→-- the asymptotic variants should have a degree of optimality or non-triviality to it.
    55→/-- Let $c(N)$ be the size of the largest $A\subseteq\{1, \dots, N\}$ such that there is a function
    56→$\delta : A \to \{-1, 1\}$ such that
    57→$$
    58→  \sum_{n\in A} \frac{\delta n}{n} = 0
    59→$$
    60→and
    61→$$
    62→  \sum_{n\in A'}\frac{\delta n}{n} \neq 0
    63→$$
    64→for all non-empty $A'\subsetneq A$. What is $\Theta(c(N))$?-/
    65→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    66→theorem erdos_319.variants.isTheta (N : ℕ) (c : ℕ → ℝ)
    67→    (h : ∀ N, IsGreatest
    68→    { (#A : ℝ) | (A) (_ : A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N)
    69→      (_ : ∃ δ : ℕ → ℤˣ, ∑ n ∈ A, (δ n : ℚ) / n = 0 ∧
    70→        ∀ A' ⊂ A, A'.Nonempty → ∑ n ∈ A', (δ n : ℚ) / n ≠ 0) } (c N)) :
    71→    c =Θ[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
    72→  sorry
    73→
    74→/-- Let $c(N)$ be the size of the largest $A\subseteq\{1, \dots, N\}$ such that there is a function
    75→$\delta : A \to \{-1, 1\}$ such that
    76→$$
    77→  \sum_{n\in A} \frac{\delta n}{n} = 0
    78→$$
    79→and
    80→$$
    81→  \sum_{n\in A'}\frac{\delta n}{n} \neq 0
    82→$$
    83→for all non-empty $A'\subsetneq A$. Find the simplest $g(N)$ such that $c(N) = O(g(N)). -/
    84→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    85→theorem erdos_319.variants.isBigO (N : ℕ) (c : ℕ → ℝ)
    86→    (h : ∀ N, IsGreatest
    87→    { (#A : ℝ) | (A) (_ : A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N)
    88→      (_ : ∃ δ : ℕ → ℤˣ, ∑ n ∈ A, (δ n : ℚ) / n = 0 ∧
    89→        ∀ A' ⊂ A, A'.Nonempty → ∑ n ∈ A', (δ n : ℚ) / n ≠ 0) } (c N)) :
    90→    c =O[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
    91→  sorry
    92→
    93→/-- Let $c(N)$ be the size of the largest $A\subseteq\{1, \dots, N\}$ such that there is a function
    94→$\delta : A \to \{-1, 1\}$ such that
    95→$$
    96→  \sum_{n\in A} \frac{\delta n}{n} = 0
    97→$$
    98→and
    99→$$
   100→  \sum_{n\in A'}\frac{\delta n}{n} \neq 0
   101→$$
   102→for all non-empty $A'\subsetneq A$. Find the simplest $g(N)$ such that $c(N) = o(g(N)). -/
   103→@[category research open, AMS 5]
   104→theorem erdos_319.variants.isLittleO (N : ℕ) (c : ℕ → ℝ)
   105→    (h : ∀ N, IsGreatest
   106→    { (#A : ℝ) | (A) (_ : A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N)
   107→      (_ : ∃ δ : ℕ → ℤˣ, ∑ n ∈ A, (δ n : ℚ) / n = 0 ∧
   108→        ∀ A' ⊂ A, A'.Nonempty → ∑ n ∈ A', (δ n : ℚ) / n ≠ 0) } (c N)) :
   109→    c =o[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
   110→  sorry
   111→
   112→/-- Adenwalla has observed that a lower bound (on the maximum size of $A$) of
   113→$$
   114→  |A| \geq (1 - \frac{1}{e} + o(1))N
   115→$$
   116→follows from the main result of Croot [Cr01].
   117→
   118→[Cr01] Croot, III, Ernest S., _On unit fractions with denominators in short intervals_.
   119→Acta Arith. (2001), 99-114.
   120→-/
   121→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
   122→theorem erdos_319.variants.lb : ∃ (o : ℕ → ℝ), (o =o[atTop] (1 : ℕ → ℝ)) ∧
   123→    ∀ᶠ N in atTop, (1 - 1 / rexp 1 + o N) * N ≤ sSup { (#A : ℝ) | (A) (_ : A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N)
   124→      (_ : ∃ δ : ℕ → ℤˣ, ∑ n ∈ A, (δ n : ℚ) / n = 0 ∧
   125→        ∀ A' ⊂ A, A'.Nonempty → ∑ n ∈ A', (δ n : ℚ) / n ≠ 0) } := by
   126→  sorry
   127→
   128→end Erdos319

    18→/-!
    19→# Conjectures associated with A063880
    20→
    21→A063880 lists numbers $n$ such that $\sigma(n) = 2 \cdot \text{usigma}(n)$, where $\sigma(n)$ is the
    22→sum of all divisors and $\text{usigma}(n)$ is the sum of unitary divisors.
    23→
    24→Equivalently, these are numbers whose unitary and non-unitary divisors have equal sum.
    25→
    26→The conjectures state that all members satisfy $n \equiv 108 \pmod{216}$, and that all
    27→primitive terms (those whose proper divisors aren't in the sequence) are powerful numbers,
    28→with $108$ being the only primitive term.
    29→
    30→*References:* [A063880](https://oeis.org/A063880)
    31→-/
    32→
    33→open scoped ArithmeticFunction.sigma
    34→
    35→namespace OeisA63880
    36→
    37→/-- The set of unitary divisors of $n$: divisors $d$ such that $\gcd(d, n/d) = 1$. -/
    38→def unitaryDivisors (n : ℕ) : Finset ℕ :=
    39→  {d ∈ n.divisors | d.Coprime (n / d)}
    40→
    41→/-- The sum of unitary divisors of $n$, denoted $\text{usigma}(n)$. -/
    42→def usigma (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    43→  ∑ d ∈ unitaryDivisors n, d
    44→
    45→/-- A number $n$ is in the sequence A063880 if $\sigma(n) = 2 \cdot \text{usigma}(n)$. -/
    46→def a (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    47→  0 < n ∧ σ 1 n = 2 * usigma n
    48→
    49→/-- The set of numbers in the sequence A063880. -/
    50→def A : Set ℕ := {n | a n}
    51→
    52→/-- A term $n$ is primitive if no proper divisor of $n$ is in the sequence. -/
    53→abbrev isPrimitiveTerm (n : ℕ) : Prop := A.IsPrimitive n
    54→
    55→/-- $108$ is in the sequence A063880. -/
    56→@[category test, AMS 11]
    57→theorem a_108 : a 108 := by
    58→  refine ⟨by norm_num, ?_⟩
    59→  decide
    60→
    61→/-- $540$ is in the sequence A063880. -/
    62→@[category test, AMS 11]
    63→theorem a_540 : a 540 := by
    64→  refine ⟨by norm_num, ?_⟩
    65→  decide
    66→
    67→/-- $756$ is in the sequence A063880. -/
    68→@[category test, AMS 11]
    69→theorem a_756 : a 756 := by
    70→  refine ⟨by norm_num, ?_⟩
    71→  decide
    72→
    73→/-- $108$ is a primitive term. -/
    74→@[category test, AMS 11]
    75→theorem isPrimitiveTerm_108 : isPrimitiveTerm 108 := by
    76→  rw [isPrimitiveTerm, Set.isPrimitive_iff]
    77→  refine ⟨a_108, ?_⟩
    78→  intro d hd
    79→  have ⟨hdvd, hlt⟩ := Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd
    80→  interval_cases d <;> simp_all [A, a] <;> decide
    81→
    82→/-- All members of the sequence satisfy $n \equiv 108 \pmod{216}$. -/
    83→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    84→theorem mod_216_of_a {n : ℕ} (h : a n) : n % 216 = 108 := by
    85→  sorry
    86→
    87→/-- All primitive terms are powerful numbers. -/
    88→@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
    89→theorem powerful_of_isPrimitiveTerm {n : ℕ} (h : isPrimitiveTerm n) : n.Powerful := by
    90→  sorry
    91→
    92→/-- $108$ is the only primitive term. -/
    93→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    94→theorem unique_primitive_108 {n : ℕ} (h : isPrimitiveTerm n) : n = 108 := by
    95→  sorry
    96→
    97→/-- If $m$ is a primitive term and $s$ is squarefree with $\gcd(m, s) = 1$, then $m \cdot s$
    98→is in the sequence. -/
    99→@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
   100→theorem a_of_primitive_mul_squarefree (m s : ℕ) (hm : isPrimitiveTerm m)
   101→    (hs : Squarefree s) (hcoprime : m.Coprime s) : a (m * s) := by
   102→  sorry
   103→
   104→/-- Non-primitive terms have the form $m \cdot s$ where $m$ is primitive and $s$ is
   105→squarefree with $\gcd(m, s) = 1$. -/
   106→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   107→theorem exists_primitive_of_a {n : ℕ} (h : a n) :
   108→    ∃ m s, isPrimitiveTerm m ∧ Squarefree s ∧ m.Coprime s ∧ n = m * s := by
   109→  sorry
   110→
   111→end OeisA63880
   112→

    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 7
    22→
    23→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/7](https://www.erdosproblems.com/7)
    24→
    25→Erdős and Selfridge asked whether there exists a distinct covering system all of whose
    26→moduli are odd.
    27→
    28→[HoNi19] Hough, B. and Nielsen, N., _Solution of the minimum modulus problem for covering
    29→systems_. Ann. of Math. (2019).
    30→
    31→[BBMST22] Balister, P., Bollobás, B., Morris, R., Sahasrabudhe, J. and Tiba, M., _Covering
    32→systems, matchings, and odd covering systems_. (2022).
    33→
    34→Selfridge showed that this problem would have a positive answer if a covering system with
    35→pairwise non-divisible moduli existed (see Problem 586).
    36→-/
    37→
    38→/--
    39→Is there a distinct covering system all of whose moduli are odd?
    40→
    41→A **distinct covering system** (`StrictCoveringSystem`) is a finite collection of
    42→congruences $\{n \equiv a_i \pmod{m_i}\}$ where all moduli $m_i$ are pairwise distinct,
    43→covering every integer. The question asks whether such a system can exist with all
    44→moduli odd and at least 2.
    45→
    46→Known results:
    47→- [HoNi19] proved that in any distinct covering system, at least one modulus must be
    48→  divisible by 2 or 3. A simpler proof was given by [BBMST22], who also showed that the
    49→  lcm of any odd covering system's moduli must be divisible by 9 or 15.
    50→- [BBMST22] proved no odd distinct covering system exists if the moduli are additionally
    51→  required to be squarefree. The general odd case remains open.
    52→-/
    53→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    54→theorem erdos_7 : answer(sorry) ↔
    55→    ∃ c : StrictCoveringSystem ℤ,
    56→      ∀ i, ∃ (m : ℕ), Odd m ∧ 1 < m ∧ c.moduli i = Ideal.span {(m : ℤ)} := by
    57→  sorry
    58→
    59→/--
    60→There is no distinct covering system with all moduli odd and squarefree.
    61→
    62→This is a stronger variant of `erdos_7` where the moduli are additionally required to be
    63→squarefree. It was disproved by [BBMST22] Balister, Bollobás, Morris, Sahasrabudhe, and Tiba.
    64→-/
    65→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    66→theorem erdos_7_squarefree : ¬ ∃ c : StrictCoveringSystem ℤ,
    67→    ∀ i, ∃ (m : ℕ), Odd m ∧ Squarefree m ∧ 1 < m ∧
    68→      c.moduli i = Ideal.span {(m : ℤ)} := by
    69→  sorry
    70→

    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/650](https://www.erdosproblems.com/650)
    23→
    24→Let $f(m)$ denote the largest number guaranteed such that for every $N \geq 1$,
    25→every $m$-element subset $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$, and every $t \geq 1$,
    26→the interval $[t, t+2N)$ contains at least $f(m)$ integers each divisible by a
    27→distinct element of $A$. Erdős and Sarányi proved $f(m) \gg m^{1/2}$; is it true
    28→that $f(m) \ll m^{1/2}$?
    29→
    30→[Er95c] Erdős, P., *Problems and results in combinatorial number theory*.
    31→
    32→[ErSa59] Erdős, P. and Sarányi, J.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→namespace Erdos650
    36→
    37→/-- The divisibility matching number: given a finite set $A$ of positive integers,
    38→a starting point $t \geq 1$, and a parameter $N$, this is the maximum $r$ such that
    39→there exist $r$ distinct integers $b_1, \ldots, b_r$ in $[t, t + 2N)$ and $r$ distinct
    40→elements $a_1, \ldots, a_r \in A$ with $a_i \mid b_i$. -/
    41→noncomputable def divMatchCount (A : Finset ℕ) (t N : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    42→  sSup {r : ℕ | ∃ (b a : Fin r → ℕ),
    43→    Function.Injective b ∧ Function.Injective a ∧
    44→    (∀ i, a i ∈ A) ∧
    45→    (∀ i, t ≤ b i ∧ b i < t + 2 * N) ∧
    46→    (∀ i, a i ∣ b i)}
    47→
    48→/-- $f(m)$: the largest value guaranteed in all configurations. For every $N \geq 1$
    49→and every $m$-element subset $A$ of $\{1, \ldots, N\}$, every interval $[t, t+2N)$ with
    50→$t \geq 1$ contains at least $f(m)$ matchable integers. -/
    51→noncomputable def erdos650F (m : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    52→  sInf {c : ℕ | ∃ (N : ℕ) (A : Finset ℕ) (t : ℕ),
    53→    A.card = m ∧ (∀ a ∈ A, 1 ≤ a ∧ a ≤ N) ∧ t ≥ 1 ∧
    54→    divMatchCount A t N = c}
    55→
    56→/--
    57→Erdős Problem 650 [Er95c]:
    58→
    59→Is it true that $f(m) \ll m^{1/2}$? Erdős and Sarányi [ErSa59] proved $f(m) \gg m^{1/2}$,
    60→so this would establish $f(m) \asymp m^{1/2}$.
    61→-/
    62→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    63→theorem erdos_650 : answer(sorry) ↔
    64→    ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧
    65→    ∃ M₀ : ℕ, ∀ m : ℕ, M₀ ≤ m →
    66→      (erdos650F m : ℝ) ≤ C * Real.sqrt (m : ℝ) := by
    67→  sorry
    68→
    69→end Erdos650
    70→

    22→# Beurling primes
    23→
    24→*References:*
    25→ - [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beurling_zeta_function)

    31→/-- A sequence of real numbers `1 < a 0 < a 1 < ...` is called a set of Beurling prime numbers if
    32→it tends to infinity. -/

    35→
    36→/-- The set of Beurling integers are numbers of the form `∏ i, (a i) ^ (k i)`, where `k` has
    37→finite support. -/
    38→def BeurlingInteger (a : ℕ → ℝ) : Set ℝ :=
    39→  letI g : (ℕ →₀ ℕ) → ℝ := fun k => k.prod (fun x y => (a x) ^ y)
    40→  .range g

    42→/-- Every element of the sequence `a` is a Beurling integer. -/
    43→lemma generator_mem_beurling (a : ℕ → ℝ) (i : ℕ) : a i ∈ BeurlingInteger a :=

    45→
    46→/-- The set of Beurling integers is closed under multiplication. -/
    47→lemma mul_mem_beurling {a : ℕ → ℝ} {x y : ℝ} (hx : x ∈ BeurlingInteger a) (hy : y ∈ BeurlingInteger a) :

    49→  obtain ⟨k, rfl⟩ := hx
    50→  obtain ⟨l, rfl⟩ := hy
    51→  exact ⟨k + l, by simp [Finsupp.prod_add_index', pow_add]⟩
    52→
    53→/-- The set of Beurling integers is closed under taking powers. -/
    54→lemma pow_mem_beurling {a : ℕ → ℝ} {x : ℝ} (k : ℕ) (hx : x ∈ BeurlingInteger a) :

    56→  induction k with

    58→  | succ k ih => simpa [pow_succ'] using mul_mem_beurling hx ih

    23→for some `b : ℕ`. At `0` this property is not well defined because `b` must be `0` and `a₀` can be

    25→squarefree part of any square is `1`. -/

    20→
    21→variable {M : Type*} [AddCommMonoid M]
    22→
    23→open scoped List
    24→
    25→/-- The set of subset sums of a set `A ⊆ M`. -/
    26→def subsetSums (A : Set M) : Set M :=
    27→  {n | ∃ B : Finset M, ↑B ⊆ A ∧ n = ∑ i ∈ B, i}
    28→
    29→/-- If `A ⊆ B`, then `subsetSums A ⊆ subsetSums B`. -/
    30→@[gcongr]
    31→theorem subsetSums_mono {A B : Set M} (h : A ⊆ B) : subsetSums A ⊆ subsetSums B :=
    32→  fun _ ⟨C, hC⟩ => ⟨C, hC.1.trans h, hC.2⟩
    33→
    34→/-- The set of subset sums of a sequence `ℕ → M`, where repetition is allowed. -/
    35→def subseqSums' (A : ℕ → M) : Set M :=
    36→  {n | ∃ B : Finset ℕ, n = ∑ i ∈ B, A i}
    37→
    38→variable [Preorder M]
    39→
    40→/-- A set `A ⊆ M` is complete if every sufficiently large element of `M` is a subset sum of `A`. -/
    41→def IsAddComplete (A : Set M) : Prop :=
    42→  ∀ᶠ k in Filter.atTop, k ∈ subsetSums A
    43→
    44→/-- If `A ⊆ B` and `A` is complete, then `B` is also complete. -/
    45→@[gcongr]
    46→theorem IsAddComplete.mono {A B : Set M} (h : A ⊆ B) (ha : IsAddComplete A) : IsAddComplete B := by
    47→  filter_upwards [ha] with x hx
    48→  exact (subsetSums_mono h) hx
    49→
    50→/-- A set `A ⊆ M` is complete if every sufficiently large element of `M` is a subset sum of `A`. -/
    51→def IsAddStronglyComplete (A : Set M) : Prop :=
    52→  ∀ ⦃B : Set M⦄, B.Finite → IsAddComplete (A \ B)
    53→
    54→/-- A strongly complete set is complete. -/
    55→theorem IsAddStronglyComplete.isAddComplete {A : Set M} (hA : IsAddStronglyComplete A) :
    56→    IsAddComplete A := by simpa using hA Set.finite_empty
    57→
    58→/-- If `A ⊆ B` and `A` is strongly complete, then `B` is also strongly complete. -/
    59→theorem IsAddStronglyComplete.mono {A B : Set M} (h : A ⊆ B) (ha : IsAddStronglyComplete A) :
    60→    IsAddStronglyComplete B := fun C hC => (ha hC).mono (by grind)
    61→
    62→/-- A sequence `A` is strongly complete if `fun m => A (n + m)` is still complete for all `n`. -/
    63→def IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq (A : ℕ → M) : Prop :=
    64→  ∀ n, IsAddComplete (Set.range (fun m => A (n + m)))
    65→
    66→/-- A strongly complete sequence is complete. -/
    67→theorem IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq.isAddComplete {A : ℕ → M}
    68→    (hA : IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq A) :
    69→    IsAddComplete (Set.range A) := by simpa using hA 0
    70→
    71→open Classical in
    72→/-- If the range of a sequence `A` is strongly complete, then `A` is strongly complete. -/
    73→theorem IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq.of_isAddStronglyComplete {A : ℕ → M}
    74→    (h : IsAddStronglyComplete (.range A)) : IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq A :=
    75→  fun n => (h (Finset.finite_toSet _)).mono (A := .range A \ ((Finset.range n).image A))
    76→    (fun _ ⟨⟨y, hy⟩, q⟩ => ⟨y - n, by grind⟩)
    77→
    78→/-- If `A` is strongly complete and the preimage of each element is finite, then the range of `A`
    79→is strongly complete. -/
    80→theorem IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq.isAddStronglyComplete {A : ℕ → M}

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 858
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/858](https://www.erdosproblems.com/858)
    23→
    24→Let $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$ be such that there is no solution to $at = b$ with
    25→$a, b \in A$ and the smallest prime factor of $t$ is $> a$. Estimate the maximum of
    26→$$\frac{1}{\log N} \sum_{n \in A} \frac{1}{n}.$$
    27→
    28→Alexander [Al66] and Erdős, Sárközi, and Szemerédi [ESS68] proved that this
    29→maximum is $o(1)$ (as $N \to \infty$). This condition on $A$ is a weaker form of the
    30→usual primitive condition. If $A$ is primitive then Behrend [Be35] proved
    31→$$\frac{1}{\log N} \sum_{n \in A} \frac{1}{n} \ll \frac{1}{\sqrt{\log \log N}}.$$
    32→
    33→An example of such a set $A$ is the set of all integers in $[N^{1/2}, N]$
    34→divisible by some prime $> N^{1/2}$.
    35→
    36→[Al66] Alexander, R., *Density and sequence of subsets of integers*.
    37→
    38→[ESS68] Erdős, P., Sárközi, A., and Szemerédi, E., *On divisibility properties of
    39→sequences of integers*.
    40→
    41→[Be35] Behrend, F., *On sequences of numbers not divisible one by another*.
    42→J. London Math. Soc. **10** (1935), 42-44.
    43→-/
    44→
    45→open scoped BigOperators
    46→
    47→namespace Erdos858
    48→
    49→/-- A finite set $A$ of positive integers satisfies the *weak primitive* condition
    50→if whenever $at = b$ for $a, b \in A$ with $t > 1$, the smallest prime factor of $t$
    51→is at most $a$. -/
    52→def IsWeakPrimitive (A : Finset ℕ) : Prop :=
    53→  ∀ a ∈ A, ∀ b ∈ A, a < b → a ∣ b →
    54→    Nat.minFac (b / a) ≤ a
    55→
    56→/-- The maximum of $\sum_{n \in A} \frac{1}{n}$ over all weak-primitive subsets
    57→$A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$. -/
    58→noncomputable def weakPrimitiveMaxSum (N : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    59→  sSup {x : ℝ | ∃ A : Finset ℕ, A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N ∧
    60→    IsWeakPrimitive A ∧
    61→    x = ∑ n ∈ A, (1 : ℝ) / (n : ℝ)}
    62→
    63→/--
    64→**Erdős Problem 858** — Known result [Al66, ESS68]:
    65→
    66→The quantity $\frac{1}{\log N} \cdot \max\left\{\sum_{n \in A} \frac{1}{n}\right\}$ over
    67→weak-primitive subsets $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$ tends to $0$ as $N \to \infty$.
    68→
    69→Formulated as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N_0$ such that for all $N \geq N_0$,
    70→$\mathrm{weakPrimitiveMaxSum}(N) \leq \varepsilon \cdot \log N$.
    71→-/
    72→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    73→theorem erdos_858 :
    74→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    75→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ N : ℕ, N ≥ N₀ →
    76→      weakPrimitiveMaxSum N ≤ ε * Real.log (N : ℝ) := by
    77→  sorry
    78→
    79→/--
    80→**Erdős Problem 858** — Behrend-type conjecture:
    81→
    82→Does there exist $C > 0$ such that for all sufficiently large $N$,
    83→$$\frac{1}{\log N} \cdot \max\left\{\sum_{n \in A} \frac{1}{n}\right\}
    84→  \leq \frac{C}{\sqrt{\log \log N}}?$$
    85→-/
    86→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    87→theorem erdos_858.variants.behrend_type :
    88→    answer(sorry) ↔
    89→    (∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    90→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ N : ℕ, N ≥ N₀ →
    91→      weakPrimitiveMaxSum N ≤ C * Real.log (N : ℝ) /
    92→        Real.sqrt (Real.log (Real.log (N : ℝ)))) := by
    93→  sorry
    94→
    95→end Erdos858
    96→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 854
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/854](https://www.erdosproblems.com/854)
    23→
    24→Let $n_k$ denote the $k$-th primorial, i.e. the product of the first $k$ primes.
    25→If $1 = a_1 < a_2 < \cdots < a_{\varphi(n_k)} = n_k - 1$ is the sequence of integers
    26→coprime to $n_k$, are there $\gg \max_i (a_{i+1} - a_i)$ many even integers
    27→of the form $a_{j+1} - a_j$?
    28→
    29→This was asked by Erdős in Oberwolfach (most likely in 1986) [Er85c, Ob1].
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Nat Finset
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos854
    35→
    36→/-- The $k$-th primorial: product of the first $k$ primes.
    37→$\operatorname{primorial}(0) = 1$, $\operatorname{primorial}(1) = 2$,
    38→$\operatorname{primorial}(2) = 6$, $\operatorname{primorial}(3) = 30$. -/
    39→noncomputable def primorial : ℕ → ℕ
    40→  | 0 => 1
    41→  | k + 1 => Nat.nth Nat.Prime k * primorial k
    42→
    43→/-- The sorted list of integers in $\{1, \ldots, n-1\}$ coprime to $n$. -/
    44→noncomputable def coprimeList (n : ℕ) : List ℕ :=
    45→  ((Finset.range n).filter (fun a => 0 < a ∧ Nat.Coprime a n)).sort (· ≤ ·)
    46→
    47→/-- Consecutive differences of a sorted list of natural numbers. -/
    48→def consecutiveDiffs : List ℕ → List ℕ
    49→  | [] => []
    50→  | [_] => []
    51→  | a :: b :: rest => (b - a) :: consecutiveDiffs (b :: rest)
    52→
    53→/-- The set of distinct gap values in the coprime sequence for $n$. -/
    54→noncomputable def gapValues (n : ℕ) : Finset ℕ :=
    55→  (consecutiveDiffs (coprimeList n)).toFinset
    56→
    57→/-- The maximum consecutive gap in the coprime sequence for $n$. -/
    58→noncomputable def maxGap (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    59→  (consecutiveDiffs (coprimeList n)).foldl max 0
    60→
    61→/--
    62→Erdős Problem 854 [Er85c, Ob1]:
    63→
    64→Let $n_k$ be the $k$-th primorial. The number of distinct even integers appearing
    65→as consecutive gaps among integers coprime to $n_k$ in $\{1, \ldots, n_k - 1\}$
    66→is $\gg \max_i(a_{i+1} - a_i)$. That is, there exist $C > 0$ and $K_0$ such that for all
    67→$k \geq K_0$, the number of distinct gap values is at least $C$ times the maximum gap.
    68→-/
    69→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    70→theorem erdos_854 : answer(sorry) ↔
    71→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧ ∃ K₀ : ℕ, ∀ k : ℕ, k ≥ K₀ →
    72→      (((gapValues (primorial k)).filter (Even ·)).card : ℝ) ≥
    73→        C * (maxGap (primorial k) : ℝ) := by
    74→  sorry
    75→
    76→end Erdos854
    77→

    19→
    20→namespace Nat
    21→
    22→/-- The greatest prime divisor of a natural number `n > 1`.
    23→
    24→Takes the junk value `0` for `n = 0` and `n = 1`.
    25→
    26→TODO Consider redefining this using the computable implementation:
    27→`n.primeFactorsList.getLastI`. -/
    28→noncomputable def maxPrimeFac (n : ℕ) : ℕ := sSup {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n}
    29→
    30→@[simp]
    31→lemma maxPrimeFac_zero :
    32→    maxPrimeFac 0 = 0 := by
    33→  simpa [maxPrimeFac] using Set.Infinite.Nat.sSup_eq_zero infinite_setOf_prime
    34→
    35→@[simp]
    36→lemma maxPrimeFac_one :
    37→    maxPrimeFac 1 = 0 := by
    38→  suffices {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p = 1} = ∅ by simp [maxPrimeFac, this]
    39→  aesop
    40→
    41→lemma prime_maxPrimeFac_of_one_lt (n : ℕ) (h : 1 < n) :
    42→    Prime (maxPrimeFac n) := by
    43→  set s := {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n} with hs
    44→  have hs₀ : s.Nonempty := by
    45→    simp only [Set.Nonempty, Set.mem_setOf_eq, ← ne_one_iff_exists_prime_dvd, hs]
    46→    omega
    47→  have hs₁ : BddAbove s := by
    48→    use n
    49→    simp only [hs, mem_upperBounds, Set.mem_setOf_eq, and_imp]
    50→    exact fun p _ hp ↦ Nat.le_of_dvd (zero_lt_of_lt h) hp
    51→  exact (Nat.sSup_mem hs₀ hs₁).1
    52→
    53→lemma maxPrimeFac_eq_of_dvd_of_le
    54→    (n p : ℕ) (hn : 0 < n) (hp : p.Prime) (h_dvd : p ∣ n) (h_le : maxPrimeFac n ≤ p) :
    55→    maxPrimeFac n = p := by
    56→  refine le_antisymm h_le ?_
    57→  replace hn : 1 < n := Nat.lt_of_lt_of_le hp.one_lt (Nat.le_of_dvd hn h_dvd)
    58→  set s := {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n} with hs
    59→  have hs₁ : BddAbove s := by
    60→    use n
    61→    simp only [hs, mem_upperBounds, Set.mem_setOf_eq, and_imp]
    62→    exact fun p _ hp ↦ Nat.le_of_dvd (zero_lt_of_lt hn) hp
    63→  exact ConditionallyCompleteLattice.le_csSup s p hs₁ ⟨hp, h_dvd⟩
    64→
    65→@[simp]
    66→lemma one_lt_maxPrimeFac_iff (n : ℕ) :
    67→    1 < maxPrimeFac n ↔ 1 < n := by
    68→  rcases lt_trichotomy n 1 with hn | rfl | hn
    69→  · simp only [lt_one_iff] at hn
    70→    simp [hn]
    71→  · simp
    72→  · simpa [hn] using (prime_maxPrimeFac_of_one_lt n hn).one_lt
    73→

import Mathlib.Data.Nat.Squarefree

/-!
# Primitive elements of a set with respect to divisibility

An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ⊆ ℕ` if `n ∈ S` and no proper divisor of `n` is in `S`.

This concept appears naturally when studying sequences closed under "divisor inheritance" -
if every member `n` of a sequence has the property that `m * n` is also in the sequence
for coprime `m`, then the primitive elements generate the entire sequence.

## Main definitions

* `Set.IsPrimitive`: A natural number `n` is primitive in `S` if `n ∈ S` and
  `n.properDivisors` is disjoint from `S`.
* `Set.primitives`: The set of primitive elements of `S`.

## Main results

* `Set.IsPrimitive.mem`: A primitive element is in the set.
* `Set.IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor`: Proper divisors of a primitive element are not
  in the set.
* `Set.one_isPrimitive_iff`: `1` is primitive in `S` iff `1 ∈ S`.
* `Set.prime_isPrimitive_iff`: A prime `p` is primitive in `S` iff `p ∈ S` and `1 ∉ S`.
-/

namespace Set

/-- An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ⊆ ℕ` if `n ∈ S` and no proper divisor of `n`
is in `S`. -/
def IsPrimitive (S : Set ℕ) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
  n ∈ S ∧ Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set ℕ) S

/-- The set of primitive elements of `S`. -/
